top_emotional_moments.text: We're coming up on the two-year anniversary
That's the episode where tonight's guest came on
he's had quite a few new experiences,
Of course, I'm talking about Paul Chafin.
Well, it's great talking with you.
Paul, for the listeners who missed that show,

segments.text: Thank you.
Thank you.
We're coming up on the two-year anniversary
of episode 354.
That's the episode where tonight's guest came on
and told us about his first dogman encounter.
Since that show aired,
he's had quite a few new experiences,
and that's what he's come back to talk about.
Of course, I'm talking about Paul Chafin.
Paul, thanks so much for coming back.
Great to be here, Vic.
Good talking to you.
Well, it's great talking with you.
I really appreciate your time.
Paul, for the listeners who missed that show,
please tell them about yourself
and give them a rundown on that first encounter you had.
I went deer hunting when I was 15 years old.
I got up late one day and almost didn't go.
Just a dark, gloomy day in November, in between the 1st and the 5th of November.
I just got up late and almost didn't go.
My dad was going to go with me, and he backed out and didn't go.
But I sat around and talked myself into going around, I think, 10, 11 o'clock, maybe 12.
and I walked way back up on the mountain and went to an area that I didn't really frequent that much
and I got back there and I just fell asleep up against a tree and when I woke up it was
it was around three or four o'clock and then around that time especially in the mountains
it gets dark a lot earlier you know the sun goes down which the sun went out but
it gets dark a lot earlier but yeah I sat there and fell asleep and when I got up I just
I kind of panicked a little bit because I knew I wasn't going to make it home before dark
because usually at dark when I'm 5, 530 and I was farther than an hour from the house
but I took about 15 steps I would say and uh fear come over me that I can't explain and
yeah it just was a primal fear and I stood there I had my hat pulled low and I kind of stood there
had a shotgun with me I had three inch slug mags in it just deer hunting everything I see
in the mountains is going to be close shot, brush shot.
I was on top of a ridge, so I was coming down into what they call a gap.
And there was a logging road that came from my right over it,
and it crossed through the gap and went around the other side of the ridge
on my left.
And I caught something walking that.
It was walking that road.
When I seen it, it was on that road.
And it looked like a black blob to me.
It was blacker than black.
I don't know if you know what phantablack is,
but if you've ever seen it, it's just black.
And it was so black that I couldn't make out details on it.
But as I watched it, I started to be able to see things like it's,
I noticed its elbow, and then I noticed that it had a chest from the side profile.
It had a tail.
It had ears that kind of pointed up, but kind of back.
I don't know if it was doing that on purpose or if that's just the way it looked.
But, yeah, it stood about 7, 8 feet tall, probably 600, 700 pounds.
And it never looked at me.
It never made eye contact.
It just walked at a steady pace from about 70 yards away.
And it still had about 70 yards before it got out of my sight.
And it went over the hill.
But, no, it never looked at me, thank God, because I don't know what I would have done.
Shooting it never crossed my mind.
I probably would have dropped the gun and run.
But I know not to run from predators and all that.
I was raised in the mountains.
But, yeah, this was a different predator.
So, yeah, I mean, that's it in a nutshell.
Well, it changed my life for sure.
You know, I left the mountain that day a different person.
I live in a different reality than everybody else that hasn seen one of these things Yeah it was pre Nobody believed me so I really put a bad taste in my mouth for talking about it for a long time
Well, I can understand why it did.
And like you said, thank goodness it didn't look at you.
You said you're in the mountains, but what mountains were they?
Where were you?
I was in eastern Kentucky in Pike County in a little place called Canada, Kentucky.
I lived at a place called Blackberry, and then, you know, my mom and dad were divorced, so I went back and forth there.
So, you know, I was in Canada, Kentucky at this time.
And in the Appalachian Mountains, as far east in Kentucky as you can go, literally, where the Hatfield-McCoy feud happened.
Researching dogmen the way you do, I don't need to tell you about how many dogmen are in that area.
And like I said before, I never got the feeling that that thing lived there.
I don't know why I got that feeling
but I just didn't
it almost like it was just passing through
and I think it stopped me
or something because
just thinking back and looking at it in my mind
and I kept walking
at the pace I was walking
if we were both blind we would have literally run into each other
so I think
it stopped me I think it seen me
it just didn't look at me I don't think it cared
because I was
140 pounds soaking wet
15 years old.
So I don't think it was too worried about me.
Thank goodness you didn't bump into him.
I think about now looking back,
it got me thinking about it.
It makes my voice shake and stuff like that.
It's traumatizing for sure.
Oh, I'm sure it does make your voice shake.
Yeah, that's not going to give you the warm and fuzzies.
Back when we did that first show,
your favorite place to research was the Daniel Boone National Forest.
Is that still your favorite place to go look for them?
It is, but I have a hard time finding somebody that will, which, of course, I've got kids and, you know, I'm a dad.
So I got family. It's hard to find somebody that will dedicate the time that I'm willing to dedicate and that has the health to go in and do what I do.
You know, because when I go, I go. I'm not trying to just walk around in the parking lot. I'm trying to go.
So, yeah, it's hard to find people that wants to go do that.
But, you know, every now and then I'll find somebody and we go.
But mainly I go down here near my house.
I've had a lot of encounters right here near home.
I'll tell you what, Paul, I've spoken with a lot of dogman researchers, and I've got to give you credit.
You're all business when you head out.
You don't mess around.
You're serious about this.
Yeah, I'm always trying to improve.
The pictures still aren't, you know, the pictures that we get and the evidence that we get.
You know, I've got a lot of track casts, a lot of really large canine track casts.
I've went into a lot of areas that I probably had no business being in.
I've been growled at.
I've had a lot of encounters.
I've been bluff charged.
I just keep sticking my nose in those areas, of course.
But that's what I go down there for.
So that's what I'm looking for is that I want to be able to show somebody.
You know, when I tell the story, and it all, I guess, probably stems back to my dad and him laughing at me when I told him.
had I been able to show a picture of what I've seen, it would have been different.
You mentioned the pictures of footprints,
and you've got your fair share of photos of dogmen themselves.
You get results when you go out.
What do you think it is about how you conduct yourself out there
that is responsible for you getting so much good evidence?
You know, that's what I tell people.
The good pictures and stuff are just right on the other side of that fight or flight feeling,
you know like because that feeling will come over you you go down into the areas that i go into
you go three times at least once you're going to come across that feeling that you need to get out
of there and if you stay you know you'll end up getting something on camera whether you get
growled at or something thrown at you or something just gets mad and stomps around something will
happen not many people are going to have the nerve to stay there and stay put when all that's going
on and get that great photo.
So you must have a lot of sin.
I'm telling you.
No, not a lot of people will.
Even the people that have been with me when I went down there, they don't, they'll go
back if I really, really need them to, but they don't really want to go back.
You know, and I guess I don't blame them if I wasn't looking for the answers I'm looking
I probably wouldn't go back either.
After talking about all the scary experiences that you've had with some of those people,
I can understand why they would want to come back.
if you've had a dogman encounter and would like to speak with me about it whether in private or on the show
please go to dogmanencounters.com and submit a report
if you've had a bigfoot sighting and would like to be a guest on one of my two bigfoot shows
please go to mybigfootsighting.com and let me know
alright Paul since you on episode 354 you had several new experiences Please talk us through those now Well I met Kyle from episode 363 about a year and a half ago two years ago
And I went up with him and met him and we went out fishing on his boat.
You know, he showed me where he had the encounters and stuff and he took me fishing.
Well, we got out on the boat.
we were on our way back for the night headed toward the boat dock and he was just trolling
along we weren't it wasn't loud or anything and we just heard a racket like I mean just
branches being broke and stuff it was off to our left it's probably about 100 yards to the shore
to our left and we were like in a little like a little inlet like I guess like a peninsula or
something I don't know what you would call it but you know we were probably about 100 yards from the
dock and it was probably about 100 yards to our left the shore was but whatever it was come off
the mountain and it was breaking stuff and it was it sounded like it was mad whatever it was but
it hit the water whatever it was i mean we found out later what it was you know it came off that
bank and hit the water and came right toward the boat so i stood up pulled the firearm and was
standing there he was kind of freaking out too which his encounter i don't blame him but i had
my firearm pulled and was standing there
ready. I was ready to shoot rounds
into the water, but yeah,
it ended up being ducks,
I've never seen ducks at 12,
1 o'clock in the morning come out like that, come out
of the woods and out of the trees and stuff.
It wasn't even in trees. They were in the woods. They come
off the ground, so they
hit the water hard, too. It sounded
like a grown man run down and hit the water.
So yeah, that's
just one that happened
shortly right after I did that interview,
and then I've been growled at near my house at this boat dock on the river, on the Kentucky River.
Yeah, I went down, and the day before I had went down, and I walked out, took my backpack off,
because there's a creek that runs out to the river, but it runs right through this river bottom.
So I walked out to the point there to where I would have had to have jumped over to the other side.
And at that time, you could. The water wasn't up high.
but I stopped on that little point on the sandbar and was pulling out my stuff getting my camera
and stuff ready you know attaching the stuff to my phone and I looked up and there was a dog man
I'm assuming it was a dog man you know they all look very strange none of them look like you think
they would but it was on all fours and it was walking down the beach on the other side looking
at me and it didn't look like it was too concerning that I was there but it had a mountain line look
and had that black, you know, that went down around its nose and stuff,
but it had big, tall ears that stuck up about six or seven inches.
And it was just a big body.
I'd say it probably weighed 250 pounds.
But, yeah, I didn't have nothing ready.
I was standing there holding all my stuff in my hands just looking at it.
And it made a left, went up the bank, and then into the tree line.
But that same day, I got some really good pictures.
and I went home
and I was showing one of my friends
the pictures and he was intrigued.
He didn't believe me.
He thought I was crazy
talking about these things.
So he wanted to go back with me
after seeing those pictures.
Well, the next day I got out of the car
and got everything ready then.
I was ready to go.
I had everything set up.
That way when I got out there,
all I had to do was hit record.
That way they didn't catch me slipping again.
So I got out there and hit record
and I seen something over in that creek.
It was about 15 foot probably
down to the creek.
I could see the top of something's head.
I told him,
I was like, Mike, come here.
I said it real quiet just like that.
I said, Mike, come here.
We were walking on sand.
Whatever was there didn't hear us walk in.
It was a very quiet,
really quiet day.
He took about three steps toward me
from my left and something growled at us
from my right.
now in the video that I have you know you can hear it really well and uh if you watch it looks like I
ran but I really didn't run I just dropped the camera from it was on a monopod or whatever so
I dropped it from vertical down to horizontal and I pulled my firearm and backed up about 30 steps
and I stood there and kept recording now I didn't get whatever it was on camera or if I did I just
wasn't able to see it but uh yeah it was very frightened him it frightened him pretty good
And it just shocked me more than anything because, in my mind, that's what I'm there for.
So it may as well have stood up and waved at me the way I look at it.
It let me know, you know, I know why you're here is pretty much the way I took it.
Then I think I talked about, you know, being bluff charged down here, just preserved.

So I mean I had things thrown at me down here hard too and been hit like whatever it was it was very accurate Whatever it was throwing sticks and stuff and rocks And we had I sure everybody that listening probably a lot of the guys probably deer hunt and have startled a deer
If you walk up on a big buck during the rut and it spots you, it'll blow really hard and then run off.
That's its way of saying, you know, I see you and letting everything else in the woods know you're there.
but uh whatever it was it didn't sound just like a deer but that's the only thing i can compare it to
it was blowing really hard and pacing back and forth about 30 yards to the right and about 30
yards back to the left and it was stomping and breaking branches and it was just blowing whatever
it was was very angry that we were there and it was throwing things and it was just out of eyesight
too and i was afraid to approach any closer because it whatever it was very magic so i mean
We've had just quite a few little encounters like that.
Not many, thankfully, have we had any that we were in real danger.
We've had a couple where it could have been really bad.
I didn't experience any infrasound by whatever growled,
but now I can feel it in my chest for sure.
Whatever it was that growled was close, and it was very large.
I've listened to just a bunch of different growls over the last year and a half.
I've listened to tigers and lions, and I've listened to bears,
just trying to figure out what it came close to.
Probably a lion, an African lion or a tiger,
probably is closest that I've heard that matches the sound,
the deep growl that it came.
It wasn't from there.
Whatever that was, was not from Kentucky.
It was not native or something we know about.
I'd say it's a safe bet that it wasn't.
No, it wasn't. It wasn't from Kentucky.
Or if it was, it wasn't nothing that's in any book I've ever seen or read.
Yeah, I'd say not.
You've got a YouTube channel of your own where you post a lot of the pics and videos that you were just talking about.
What's your channel's name?
It's the Kentucky Dogman Project.
It's fairly new. I've only had the channel, the YouTube channel.
I've got a Facebook group, Kentucky Dogman Project.
but the YouTube channel is only about four months old probably,
and I've got quite a few videos on there.
So, I mean, anybody wants to go check that out, it's well worth it.
There's a bunch of short videos on there too.
So, I mean, I've gathered a lot of evidence over the years.
You know, I've done a lot of research with a lot of big names like Renee Wilson.
I think she's been on your channel before.
I've researched with her.
I've researched with Kurt Stokes.
I've been out with a few people
I've met a lot of good people and had a lot of good times
doing this
I mean for it to have been such a
stress on my life
being able to go out with people
that's had similar encounters and stuff
it's really helped me out a lot
over the years so
I don't want to say I enjoy it
but it's fun doing it with
somebody like my girlfriend
who totally understands everything
and she knows I'm not lying
She's seen the things and she's pulled thorns out of my head where I tried to climb a thorn tree after I got bluff charged.
So, yeah, she knows I'm not kidding around.
Was it a hawthorn tree you tried to climb?
Well, the tree didn't have thorns on it, but there was a vine growing around it that had one inch thorns.
They were big, sharp ones, too.
I heard a dog bark and it sounded like it was about 50, 60 yards away.
and I didn't when it barked I thought man that's a big dog you know like I thought wow and then
when it barked again it sounded closer to me and it sounded louder I've never heard anything like
this and then it barked again and was closer so like I said I had that shotgun with me because
that's usually what I carry and I was alone of course like I shouldn't have been but uh the first
thing that hit my mind was there was somebody down there with a dog because I was in a nature
Preserve and there was a hiking trail in the area but it wasn't a well-known like a lot of people
don't know about it but uh the name of it's the Jim Beam Nature Preserve you can look it up you
know it ain't a secret now now you can't go down there they got a gate up I don't know why they
have that gate up but they do but uh whatever that was was coming fast and hard through the
trees and I was standing in the opening like in a field kind of and I was about 30 yards 20 30
yards from the tree line and it was coming fast so I turned around and tried to climb that tree
because in my mind all I could picture was what one of those slug mags would do to somebody's
domestic dog and I didn't want to have to explain that so I was going to hit that tree and try to
climb it but that tree for one wouldn't have ever held me without the thorns but those thorns was
dug in my hand and this while me and you were doing the last interview a couple years ago I
had those thorns in my head and in my hand at that time I hadn't had them dug out yet because
It just happened a day prior.
but whatever it was came right to that tree line and dead stopped,
and it never made another sound because I spun around with that shotgun
because I knew I couldn't get that tree, and I was just going to,
whatever it was, was about to eat some lead.
I made peace with it and was going to blast it off this earth,
but it stopped dead, and it didn't come out,
and I backtracked the way I come in and eased my way out of there.
I thought, when I get to the parking lot, I'll find out who's here.
I got there and there wasn't nobody there but my car.
So, yeah, it was pretty scary.
I'm sure it was.
Yeah, sounds like it.
And one-inch thorns, that's no joke.
Yeah, not at all.
Of all the times you've been in their presence,
which encounter would you point to as being the most frightening one?
That one, that bluff charge.
Because I just, I went over it fairly quick this night,
but I'm pretty sure I talked about it in the last interview.
but that one was coming toward me, you know, like the other one I seen and it was walking away from me, you know,
and even though it was scary, it just wasn't like this one, this one here.
I knew in my mind I'm about to have to deal with this.
You know, there ain't no getting away from it.
This is coming right at me and I'm going to have to deal with it.
So, yeah, it was a different kind of fear.
Whatever it was, I'm sure if it's got any intelligence, it had to have got a kick out of what it done to me.
So it scared me.
Like you walk up on a big rattlesnake in the woods or something and get too close before you see it, your knees will get weak.
And that's kind of how I felt.
It was definitely scary.
You know, for me to try to climb that thorn bush, I don't know what got into me.
I should have just dealt with it.
However, but yeah, I didn't.
I dealt with it wrong.
I got a good look at myself and what not to do.
And I realized that something may go wrong one of these times and I may have to deal with it.
So, you know, it was an eye-opening experience.
Yeah, that time was probably the most scary.
Everyone's got a plan until they get hit.
Sounds like you got hit that day.
Yeah, I think that's what you said last time, Dick.
I think that's what you said.
Mark Tyson said that.
Everybody's got a plan until they get punched in the mouth.
That's right.
Yeah, they normally do.
From what I understand, Paul, your views regarding their capabilities have changed.
Please expand on that for us.
Well, the last interview we did, I was certain that these were flesh and blood.
I used to laugh at people who talked about cloaking and who talked about the woo factor of all this stuff.
And, you know, I just laughed at all that stuff because I was finding tracks.
I was finding scat.
I was finding every time I would see one on video, they would have gnats around their face like a normal dog.
And they just, everything I had been seeing up until that point was flesh and blood is what it led me to believe.
And then I started getting better equipment to record with and just better stuff.
And I started learning more as I went what to look for when I was watching video and, you know, reviewing footage.
And I started seeing orbs.
And I say orbs because you can tell the difference because when a bug goes by a leaf, it don't light it up.
You know, these orbs, they were literally lighting the leaves up as they went by them and stuff.
And I started seeing cloaked.
They were cloaked in the picture quality of the video that I had because my phone, I got an iPhone and you can put it on 4K and improve the picture.
And I also had a monocular that attaches to, I'm sure you've seen them advertised, but it attaches to your phone.
So I had that on there as well.
So the picture quality was just insanely good.
So you could just see the distortion that they caused and how they made the grass behind them blurry.
And I just started seeing some that had heads.
You could see them in the trees, like from a distance, you could see them in the trees and their heads look like they were as big as the hood of a car.
You know, and I just know that there's no possible way in real life that something like that's there.
so I just started seeing a lot of things
which I'm sure most researchers will ignore
because they don't want to go that route
or they don't want to acknowledge it
and talk about it
and get involved in that
which I understand
but I won't do that
I'm going to tell you what I see
I'm going to show it to you
I may not like it
but that's just the reality of it
there is a supernatural side to this
they are flesh and blood ones too
I also believe
but there is a supernatural side
whether they're in a different dimension
or a realm or their death.
I don't know, you know,
but they are in a different dimension,
I think, personally.
But I think it overlays ours.
Like I used to,
when I thought of a dimension,
I would think of it as something far off
instead of a dimension
that overlaid our dimension, you know?
So I've just came,
I came a long way and talked to a lot of different people And I sat with Scott Carpenter for probably an hour at a convention I got to sit and talk to him Just me and him sat and talked
He had seen my videos and pictures and stuff.
He knew who I was.
I've got to talk to him and just a few people that are well-known in the field.
I've got to sit and pick their brains and stuff.
I've been very fortunate when it comes to that.
It sure sounds like you have been.
There's a place called Corbin in Kentucky that holds a unique distinction for you
When it comes to dog men, please talk us through that.
Well, the Corbin areas, now that's where Kurt Stokes has done a lot of research and stuff
and has pulled a lot of video and evidence out there that has made him known.
You know, like it's the area, you couldn't find it really unless, you know,
somebody pointed it out to you or told you about it.
But it's in that area of southeast Kentucky, you know, right in the foothills.
but it's a national park.
It's right on the outskirts.
You pass a Daniel Boone National Forest sign, and then, you know,
it's just barely inside the park.
So it's just an eerie place to me.
When I go in there, I can just feel it, that the ground is, you know,
they say the Indians wouldn't stay there.
They would just hunt and move on.
So there's a reason for that.
And if I could tell you some of the names of the place,
you would understand what I'm talking about.
But yeah, it's just a different place.
It's got a different feel from any other place I've ever been.
And if you know who Kurt Stokes is and have watched his videos,
then you understand.
What do you think it is about that area that makes it so attractive to dogmen?
I think it ain't just that place.
I think it's just all national parks that's protected by the government
or whoever, the National Park Service or whoever.
It's just protected land.
You know, so I think they just have, I don't know, I think they feel safe there maybe.
Or maybe it has something to do with the granite or the limestone because it's all cliffs and caves and overhangs.
So there's a lot of good reasons to be there if you are a dogman.
If I was a dogman, that's where I'd be.
Yeah, it's just really rough terrain.
You've got to be in good shape to go any distance there.
You've got to be in good shape.
Does sound like an awfully creepy place.
No wonder they like it so much.
How far do you live from the LBL, Paul?
I live right in the center of the state right now.
I live just south of Lexington, about 15 minutes.
So I live about three hours, four hours from Lamb Twin Lakes.
That's pretty far.
Have you done any research there?
No, I haven't.
I haven't got a chance to go down there.
I would have to have somebody go with me.
I've heard a lot of people talk about also the Mammoth Cave area
and it being really like a really big hot spot.
I've heard quite a few tales come out of there that are, you know,
situations I wouldn't want to be in.
Yeah, definitely.
I can relate to that.
I've heard some pretty creepy stories.
Do you have any opinions on the murders that allegedly happened in the LBL?
I do.
I don't personally believe it.
I don't believe any of the stories.
I believe, I mean, I don't think anybody knows the real truth.
I think the family was probably killed there, and it probably was a cryptid that done it.
But I don't think anybody knows.
I don't think anybody was there and survived.
Maybe the police officers did go back and tell, you know, maybe a couple of these other big-name researchers do know something that everybody else don't.
But I personally don't believe that anybody survived and lived to tell about it 30 years later or 40 years later.
It really does make you wonder if there's something behind those stories.
You never know.
Well, people want to believe that stuff.
People want a good story.
Maybe I'm wrong.
Yeah, it's hard to say.
I guess we're never going to know.
Having had as many run-ins with Dogmen as you have over the years,
do you think how dangerous they are is oversold,
or do you think it's actually accurate when people represent them as being kill-you-on-sight monsters?
I think they will warn you
and if you ignore those warnings
something bad could happen to you
I think they're probably
diverse just like everybody else
you might run up on one that's lost it's mom
or it's girl or it's kid
or anything could have happened to it
it could be having a bad day you know you never know
you know it could have been shot at
you're carrying a gun in it's home you know
so you just never know what you're going to run up on
but I do believe they will give you a warning
and if you don't listen or
take heat and David Pilates would be talking about you.
You raised some good points.
You never know, it might have gotten up on the wrong side of the log that morning.
It's hard to say.
The fact you go out looking for them says you have a certain level of comfort
being there at presence.

Does that mean you still do everything in the woods you used to do before you found out about them being out there Well where I from we used to do a lot of riverbank fishing you know in the Tug River which separates West Virginia from Kentucky
You know, my dad was a really big fisherman and deer hunter and stuff, and so am I.
I still am.
But I can't really enjoy myself and sit with my back to the tree line anymore.
I'm always looking over my shoulder, and I'm more of a bass fisherman now in the daytime.
I don't really like going down to the river at night and sitting like I used to.
It just ain't the same anymore.
Even deer hunting anymore, you know, I can deer hunt, but it's always in the back of my mind.
You know, like I said before, it'll always be there every day.
It was there even the days my kids were born.
You know, I got three kids.
You know, every day it's in my mind.
You know, there's never a day that goes by where I'm not thinking about it.
Unfortunately, there's life before you knew they were out there and life after.
